appeal_letter:
Dear UnitedHealthcare Appeals Review,

I am writing to appeal the denial of continued Home Health Care services for [Member Name] from 05/14/2026 to 05/28/2026. The denial states that the extension was not prior authorized before services were rendered. This appeal asks you to review the clinical necessity of those services and to grant retroactive authorization.

The patient is 78 years old, lives alone, and was discharged from the hospital with a wound requiring daily nursing care. During the third week of home health services, the patient developed a wound infection requiring daily dressing changes by a skilled nurse. The patient is homebound — she cannot travel to an outpatient clinic for wound care without clinical risk — and the services provided during the disputed period were skilled nursing services that meet UHC's definition of covered home health care.

The extension authorization was not obtained before the third week because the home health agency missed the reauthorization deadline — an administrative failure on the agency's part, not a clinical one. The patient had no knowledge of the administrative requirement and no ability to ensure it was met. Denying coverage for services that were medically necessary and rendered in good faith, on the basis of an agency administrative error, places the financial burden of that error on a vulnerable patient.

We ask that UHC consider retroactive authorization for the services rendered, recognizing that the clinical criteria for continued home health care — homebound status and the need for skilled intermittent care — were met throughout the disputed period.

We request reversal of this denial. Please confirm receipt and the deadline for any further submission.

This letter is a draft for your review.

feature_scores.evidence: Homebound status and skilled nursing need are cited but not yet supported with attached documentation.
Correctly frames the agency error as separate from the patient's clinical eligibility, and requests retroactive authorization.
Wound infection and daily dressing requirement are stated; clinical notes supporting these facts would strengthen the case.

gaps: moderate: grounding (anchor 3) — nursing visit notes and the wound infection documentation are needed to support the retroactive authorization request
outcome.critique: Good framing of the agency-error-vs-patient-eligibility distinction; supporting clinical documentation is the key remaining need.
rationale: Retroactive authorization for medically necessary services where the patient was not at fault is the standard equitable remedy.
Wound infection with homebound patient is a strong clinical necessity argument.

risk_flags: UHC may require the home health agency to submit an explanation of the missed deadline; coordinate with the agency before filing.

mirror.what_was_denied: Continued Home Health Care services (nursing and physical therapy) from 05/14/2026 to 05/28/2026.
mirror.why_they_said_no: They say the continuation was not prior authorized — the initial two-week episode was approved but the extension request was never filed.
mirror.deadline_note: Check your denial letter for the appeal filing deadline and submit before that date.
mirror.strongest_angle: The home health agency missed the reauthorization deadline — that is an administrative error by the agency, not by you, and the services provided were medically necessary throughout.

citations_used.title: Denial letter
citations_used.quote: Any continued care beyond the initial episode requires a new prior authorization request with updated clinical notes demonstrating that you remain 'homebound' and require intermittent skilled care

missing_evidence_checklist: Nursing visit notes from the disputed period documenting wound infection and daily dressing changes
Statement from the home health agency acknowledging the missed reauthorization deadline
Physician's note confirming homebound status and the ongoing need for skilled nursing care
